author: @JohnJenkins-b7e
text:
What a wonderful tool. Who knew we could turn tires and plastic into fuel.This could help clean our plastics and metals up as well. Carbon left after an updraft gasifier can extract so many things from our water and air.  I made one of these and was able to convert my downdraft gasifier into an updraft gasifier using propane tanks like Mr Teslonian. I decided to use a propaine tank as a water jacket guess that could be used for a steam boiler since the the  exhaust pipe is plumbed trough it like a boiler uses. I’m not afraid of steam boilers
 if used correctly. I saw a guy turn an air gun into a micro turbine. Low pressure is key they run well under 90 psi. I wonder what else I could use to condense the gas because over time that water would get hot. I enjoy making methane gas from my bio digesters what a brilliant way to trap that gas and use it. Guess it could be re burned into the steam boiler aka propaine tank. I decided to plumb the the exhaust line through my biodigester to accelerate methane gas production. Why not use all that waste heat then condense it back into the boiler aka propaine tank. I would assume redirecting the syngas and methane back to the boiler would make this system very efficient. I’m all about cleaning up the environment and ourselves in the process.

author: @lokiii3507
text:
Dear Robert,
I have read a published article two days ago, in Nature catalysis, called 'Microwave-initiated catalytic deconstruction of plastic waste into hydrogen and high-value carbons'. It is a breakthrough method of getting rid of the plastic waste.
One solution is the ongoing research in Japan, on extraction of polymer dissolving enzymes, which certain bacteriae have.
This solution, however, can be performed at home and with you regular microwave oven, very user friendly and requires several minutes of operating time. The byproducts of this microwave oven catalyst decomposion of certain types of polymers are nearly pure hydrogen gas (along some hydrocarbons) and pure carbon in the form of multi-walled carbon nanotubes. Both are highly useful- hydrogen gas can be saved and used as fuel, and the carbon nanotubes are applicable in industry and you name what. 
The only thing harder to obtain is a mixture of FeAlOx, which gets mixed with the ground polymer pellets. It absorbs microwaves and releases heat which causes this catalytic reaction. As far as I've gathered, to get it, one requires magnetite Fe3O4 and the mineral alumina oxide Al2O3. 

Now, I leave you with this information with hope that your intelligence will tweak the design and make it even more user friendly. Please use your great subscription list to spread this information so we might turn the tables on global polymer pollution!
Thank you for your time!

Your fan from Croatia,
L. M.

author: @ScarletFlames1
text:
Remember to not skimp on the cleaning of the gas before you shove it into the engine, it's gotta burn AT LEAST as clean as the intented fuel source otherwise it will gunk up and junk the engine, wood gas without any cleaning drips carbon residues all over the place.

Besides, we'd like to see you test the burning of the gas before and after cleaning to see the difference. I wonder if it counts as carbon neutral or even negative since quite a lot of the carbon the tree soaked up would be locked up in the filters\cleaners\dirty water.

author: @RB-yq7qv
text:
Robert 
We have tons and tons of waste food, lawn cuttings, shrubs cuttings being dumped every day which produce methane. If this gas could be compressed say used in a small industrial unit that can run small type generators mountains of money could be saved. think of a unit in a small village that is supplied by the waste of the village that then is transformed into power generation. Methane gas is the biggest killer of our planet as unlike CO2 it can not be recycled by natural systems, ( plants take up CO2 ). One of our biggest producers of methane is sewerage treatment works if this was cycled into power generation our plant would be a safer place. Mix the both and we end up with a byproduct of the greatest organic fertilizer and soil conditioner to establish more forest that can recycle CO2. As they say from something small big things grow.

author: @SheplerStudios
text:
My father-in-law who was a WWII submarine veteran (USS Cobia (SS 245)) mentioned stops in Australia for repairs, provisions and fuel and remembered the taxi drivers there had switch their cars over to gasification (coal in the instance he mentioned). Every once in a while the driver would stop, lift up the trunk hood and stoke the fire for more coal gas to the vehicle’s engine.

Here in the states, I grew up in a small city in central Illinois where one of two coal fired power plants had set up a coal gasification plant (with government subsidies) to help meet tougher enviornmental emission standards. The plant was just about to go online when George W. Bush won the 2000 presidential election over Al Gore (An Inconvenient Truth) and the new air standards were throw out and the gasification plant scrapped without ever having been fired up.

When you can use cogeneration in the gasification process (i.e. waste heat), the overall efficiency increases sharply. What I’m wondering is how do efficiencies of external combustion engines burning bio fuels directly, like in steam and Stirling examples, compare to gasification to run internal combustion engines? Could the waste heat of the gasification process hold sufficient energy to run a scavenger Stirling generator?

Absolutely love you videos and thank you so much for sharing your knowledge and curiosity.

author: @flashlit123
text:
Check out Tactical WoodGas on youtube.  Mike Leister sells a big dragon wood gasifier on his website as well as on Amazon if you have more money than time.  It is basically 4 metal buckets connected with electrical conduit.  The gas goes from the burner/producer buckets and into a radiator in a water tank (another bucket) to cool the gas and condense the water vapor and tar.  From there it goes to yet another bucket filled with something (fiberglass insulation, wood shavings, etc.) to further filter the gas and then into a plastic flexible pipe where it can go to your engine.  A 12 volt fan is used to get it started but I think it is supposed to continue running just from the vacuum of the engine.  PVC ball valves are used on the cool side to control where the gas goes as well as to set the air/fuel mixture.  A canning jar is used to catch the condensed liquid.  JB Weld or some other kind of high temp "liquid steel" is used  and metal conduit connectors instead of welding or brazing so it can be made without a lot of tools or special skills.

I bought his gasifier but haven't gotten the air/fuel mixture right to keep the engine running for extended periods yet.  When the gasifier is first started, it makes a lot of smoke until it gets working good enough to burn the gas either directly or in an engine.  I think I still have too many air leaks as well as not having the air/fuel mixture right but I hope to run a generator from wood gas eventually.  I look forward to seeing how yours turns out and get some good ideas.

This is another one of my aimed for projects, only I aim to make mine a turbine powered rig, instead of piston operated! That way I can get 100% of the combustion energy as a motor, as compared to giving up a lot of motive heat without getting the motive force from it!

Also, my design will in no way negate the value of the heat being generated, it will simply direct it into a single focal point [the high speed exhaust] where I have some fine one inch 600 psi stainless steel coils wherein I can then turn water into steam (the MUCH LESS efficient way of going about it) OR simply separate ammonia from an ammonia/water solution under a partial hydrogen atmosphere (the MUCH MORE efficient option!)

replies.author: @petermiddleton6902
replies.text: Very much!, biogas generated from food waste is a wonderful safe green fuel, with nutrient rich organic liquid fertilizers as the byproduct. Yes it certainly needs higher ambient temperatures to work effectively in a cold climate like the UK. But say if you install a longish linear flow biogas bag reactor inside a double insulated mini polytunnel, and then use a waste cellulose rich slow composting thermal generating heap under it. Then optimum rates of biogas should be produced even in the deepest of winters as well. A small addition of atomized iron powder mixed into the feed stock will also reduce unwanted oxygen, and enable higher calorific value biogas production as well (effectively reducing the CO2 content in the final biogas), and at the same time dramatically reducing the unwanted HS component percentage. Small amounts of finely powered biochar/ activated charcoal added to the feedstock will also further turbo charge biogas production. As the micro pores in the organic carbon make awesome happy party homes for the methanogenic bacteria to thrive :) (y)

text: Since human solid waste is problematic (disease transfer) to use as fertilizer, maybe that is a possible feedstock for a gasifier?

author: @lokiii3507
text:
Ahhh, good weather, Sir!
I will be going into this gasifiers soon. Only soon as I was iffy about it, not knowing whether or not it's useful and efficient. But, seeing you wanting to make one, I am sure it is! :)
A tiny house can with a rocketstove fed with wood and coal you get after gasification and a generator to fill the bottle with synth gas. And perhaps even the bio waste storage and methane! Oh how happy I get ;D
Good luck, sir!

author: @ralphsammis7330
text: Now your cooking!  Let’s remove the damaging carbons.

author: @RoadRageLive
text: You may want to stop by a local car parts shop to get a couple one-way valves. I use PCV valves. I had purchased a small supply for my old car years ago and still had a couple left over when I sold the car. I used the PCV valve to prevent back fires and placing one at the carburetor end of the system.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice tip mate - cheers

replies.author: @RoadRageLive
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering it will help you save some facial hair

author: @pion137
text: I am wondering if to recover some of the waste heat, is it worth adding peltier or thermoelectric stacks for charging your dc devices?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I would think so mate

author: @rastamanralph6670
text: Cool great minds definitely think alike😁👌 I've got a brand new wursburgh think it's called German 8500 watt 12/240/480 three phase and possibly 110 generator which I only paid £150 for and has €1196 on the box. I'm thinking of building a gasifier and have been wanting to since I watched Mr teslonian on YouTube build one years ago that also ran a fridge on the gas and made different fuels in the cooling process.he makes all kinds of energy devices a bit like you. I've never put any petrol in the generator just oil and never had it started. I know there will be power cuts in the future and I want to be having electric when this happens so your version might be something I can do with the materials I have lying about.😉🍻

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: turn it over just to make sure it starts and we can do the build together!

author: @markylyons
text: check out Colin Furse, he did the same thing with a lawnmower, clogged up the engine, needs a good filter

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I know Colin Furse's channel mate and there are a couple of tricks to stop clogging it - but I will go through them on this build as it progresses

author: @alexanderhodnefjell7920
text: Could you make and explain a buck converter?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sure mate

author: @Jay-lc1nh
text: Besides a dehumidifier is there anything else one could apply to a gasifier unit to increase efficiency for a home unit? Thermo-electric application i mean.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: for sure mate there are quite a few things you can do with them but in gasifier terms home built are pretty efficient in themselves you know

replies.author: @Jay-lc1nh
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Looking forward to the build thx.

author: @BiosWars
text: Robert,  Please consider using a rocket stove design to create the gases. then venting those into the combustion unit.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I was going to do a down draft design mate as it is less harsh on the fire box and grid - rocket stove types are updraft and they can be short lived apparently

author: @herenow2895
text: How about the power from this to run an HHO generator, which in turn could be used to generate even more power.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: for sure - but I would think that would be more for storage rather than on demand

author: @htmagic
text: RMS, I immediately thought of a wood gasifier unit. Great minds think alike! The FEMA manual was written by the Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL), Oak Ridge, TN near where I live. I have an 50 gal old water heater that I want to cut up and make into a gasifier. We have twigs and leaves all around us, plenty for a gasifier!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have been to oak ridge mate - get that container out lol!

replies.author: @htmagic
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Sadly, it's hard to do anything when you're a geographic bachelor working out of town and coming home on weekends.
